content.text: Non-functional requirements for latency and throughput are not afterthoughts to be tuned in production. For Claude integrations, they are design constraints that determine model tier selection, deployment topology, retrieval architecture, and caching strategy. An integration with a 500 ms P95 latency target requires fundamentally different architectural decisions than one with a 5-second target.

Blueprint NFRs capture these constraints in a form that the Design Review Board can evaluate during gate review. They answer three questions: What latency does the user experience? What request volume must the system sustain? What happens when the system is under stress?
Latency NFRs that are set after the architecture is designed are observations, not requirements. Set them before design: they constrain the design space.

attrs.alt: Latency budget breakdown for a RAG chat integration showing component-level P95 targets

content.text: Percentile Targets Are the Standard Unit

Latency NFRs are expressed as percentile targets, not averages. An average latency of 1 second can coexist with a P99 latency of 30 seconds — the 1% of users experiencing 30-second responses are invisible in the average. The percentiles that matter for user experience are P50, P95, and P99.
Half of requests are faster than this
Baseline user experience; the number most users will experience
95% of requests are faster than this
The de facto standard SLO target; reflects the typical 'slow day' experience
99% of requests are faster than this
Captures outliers; if P99 >> P95, the system has occasional severe slowdowns that impact 1 in 100 requests
99.9% of requests are faster than this
Only relevant for very high-volume services (> 10,000 rps); extremely sensitive to transient issues
Setting Latency Targets by Integration Type

Latency targets vary by integration type because user expectations and business consequences differ. A customer-facing chat interface has a lower tolerance for slow responses than an overnight batch processing pipeline.
Users abandon sessions after ~3s; customer impact is immediate and visible
Internal users more tolerant but productivity impact accumulates
User expects processing time; progress indicator acceptable
Throughput-gated, not latency-gated; measure total job duration instead
Multi-step tasks take longer; streaming progress to user is required above 5s

Once the overall P95 target is set, it must be broken into a per-component budget. This is the design constraint that each component owner must meet. Components with slack in their budget can absorb measurement uncertainty; components at their limit require careful monitoring.

# nfrs/latency_budget.py
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class LatencyBudget:
    # All values in milliseconds, targeting P95
    auth_routing_ms: int
    retrieval_ms: int        # vector search + reranking
    prompt_assembly_ms: int  # context concatenation, token counting
    inference_ms: int        # Claude time-to-last-token (P95)
    post_processing_ms: int  # filtering, logging

    @property
    def total_ms(self) -> int:
        return (
            self.auth_routing_ms
            + self.retrieval_ms
            + self.prompt_assembly_ms
            + self.inference_ms
            + self.post_processing_ms
        )

    def validate_against_slo(self, slo_ms: int) -> None:
        if self.total_ms > slo_ms:
            slack = self.total_ms - slo_ms
            raise ValueError(
                f"Latency budget ({self.total_ms}ms) exceeds SLO ({slo_ms}ms) "
                f"by {slack}ms. Reduce a component budget or revise the SLO."
            )

# Standard RAG chat blueprint: P95 SLO = 3000ms
RAG_CHAT_BUDGET = LatencyBudget(
    auth_routing_ms=150,
    retrieval_ms=500,
    prompt_assembly_ms=100,
    inference_ms=2100,   # dominant term — directly tied to model tier and token count
    post_processing_ms=150,
)
RAG_CHAT_BUDGET.validate_against_slo(slo_ms=3000)  # total: 3000ms — passes
Time to First Token (TTFT) as a Latency Sub-NFR

For streaming integrations, users see the first characters of the response before the full generation is complete. TTFT is the elapsed time from request receipt to the first token being streamed to the client. It determines how long the user waits before seeing any output.
Typically set at 20–30% of the total P95 latency target. For a 3-second total target, TTFT should be < 750 ms.
Auth + routing + retrieval + prompt assembly + Claude network round-trip + first token generated. Retrieval dominates for RAG integrations; prompt assembly dominates for long system prompts.
TTFT is only meaningful if the integration actually streams. Non-streaming integrations deliver no output until generation is complete — TTFT is undefined and irrelevant.

# measuring_ttft.py
import time
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def measure_ttft(prompt: str, model: str = "claude-sonnet-4-6") -> tuple[float, str]:
    start = time.monotonic()
    first_token_time = None
    full_response = []

    with client.messages.stream(
        model=model,
        max_tokens=1024,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    ) as stream:
        for text in stream.text_stream:
            if first_token_time is None:
                first_token_time = time.monotonic()
            full_response.append(text)

    ttft_ms = (first_token_time - start) * 1000
    return ttft_ms, "".join(full_response)

# In load testing, measure TTFT across N requests and compute P95
Requests Per Second vs. Tokens Per Second

Claude integrations have two distinct throughput dimensions. Requests per second (rps) measures how many API calls the system handles. Tokens per second (tps) measures the total token processing rate — a more direct proxy for Anthropic API cost and rate limit consumption.
The primary throughput metric for synchronous integrations. Set from load forecasts: expected peak concurrent users × requests per user per minute ÷ 60.

The primary throughput metric for batch integrations. Directly proportional to API cost. Claude Sonnet generates approximately 100–200 output tokens/second per stream; total tps across all concurrent workers determines API tier requirements.
Less critical than output, but relevant for context assembly latency and API rate limit headers (Anthropic rate limits are applied to input + output tokens combined).

Anthropic API rate limits are expressed in requests per minute (RPM) and tokens per minute (TPM) per API key. Blueprint throughput NFRs must be reconciled with the team's provisioned rate limit before the integration enters production.
Throughput NFR must be no more than 70% of the provisioned rate limit. The remaining 30% is headroom for burst, retries, and concurrent team usage.
When multiple integrations share a rate limit via the proxy gateway, total throughput across all integrations must stay within 70% of the provisioned limit.
Monitor the Anthropic API response headers (x-ratelimit-remaining-requests, x-ratelimit-remaining-tokens) and alert when remaining capacity drops below 20% of the provisioned limit.

Latency and throughput NFRs define steady-state requirements. Stress NFRs define what happens when the system is overloaded. Without explicit degradation requirements, the system will degrade unpredictably under load — which is worse than predictable degradation.
System must remain available; P95 latency may increase by up to 2×; no data loss
Rate limiter queues excess requests; queue depth alert at 100 items
Integration retries with backoff; user sees 'busy' state, not an error; no cascading failure
Circuit breaker + retry with jitter; fallback response if circuit open
Integration proceeds with reduced context (fewer retrieved chunks) rather than timing out
Retrieval timeout budget enforced; degraded mode returns best-available chunks
Queue absorbs excess; worker count scales up (if auto-scaling) or surplus waits; SLA timer starts at enqueue
Dead letter queue for items exceeding 2× SLA; page on-call if DLQ grows

A degradation ladder defines the sequence of quality reductions the system applies as load increases, from full service to minimal service. Each step is a concrete, testable behaviour, not a vague policy.
All features active, retrieval using max chunks, conversation history in full. P95 latency within SLO.
Triggered when retrieval P95 > 800ms. Reduce retrieved chunks from 8 to 4. Trade response quality for latency.
Triggered when retrieval P95 > 2000ms. Serve from model knowledge only, with a notice that the knowledge base is temporarily unavailable.
Triggered when Claude API P95 > 5000ms. Return cached responses for the 50 most common query patterns. Log cache hit rate and alert ops team.
Triggered when circuit breaker is open for > 60s. Return a static 'service temporarily unavailable' message with contact information.
NFRs are only useful if they are measured continuously and reported to stakeholders. The standard measurement and reporting requirements for Claude integration NFRs:

Every request must emit a histogram metric with component-level spans (auth, retrieval, inference, post-processing) using OpenTelemetry or equivalent. Histograms, not averages — percentiles cannot be computed from averages.
Requests per minute and tokens per minute, emitted as counters every 60 seconds. Separate dimensions for integration ID, model tier, and outcome (success, error, rate-limited).
Alert when the error budget is being consumed faster than the allowed rate. Standard alert: page if 1-hour burn rate > 14.4× (would exhaust 30-day budget in 2 hours).
Published to the integration owner and operations lead: P50/P95/P99 latency for the week, peak and average rps, error rate, and SLO budget remaining.

Latency and throughput NFRs for Claude integrations are design constraints that must be set before the architecture is finalised, not goals to be optimised after go-live. They are expressed as percentile targets (P50, P95, P99), decomposed into per-component budgets, aligned with API rate limit provisioning, and complemented by explicit degradation requirements that define how the system behaves under overload.

The standard measurement infrastructure — latency histograms with component-level spans, throughput counters, SLO burn rate alerts, and weekly NFR reports — ensures that compliance is continuously visible and that degradation trends are caught before they breach the SLO. NFRs that are set, measured, and acted upon are the difference between a production-ready integration and one that degrades unpredictably at scale.
